fact: June 21 is the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere - the day with the most sunlight of the entire year!
In Alaska on this date, the sun barely sets at all, creating a weird twilight that lasts almost all night long.
Down in Antarctica, June 21 is the darkest day of winter - some places get zero sunlight for the whole day!
Fireflies typically start flashing their butts like crazy around this time because the warm nights are perfect for finding mates.
This date marks when Cancer season begins in astrology - people born on June 21 are right on the cusp between Gemini and Cancer.
In northern countries, wild strawberries often ripen right around now, which is why some places call June the 'Strawberry Moon' month.
June 21 falls exactly 172 days into the year - that's almost halfway through!
Mosquitoes usually reach peak annoyance levels around this date in many places because the warm, humid weather makes them super active.
In Scandinavia, the sun can stay up for 20+ hours on June 21, making the sky look like a never-ending sunset.
Many flowers like roses and peonies typically hit their blooming peak right around now because they love the long daylight hours.
Ocean temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are still getting warmer even though this is the longest day - the water takes time to heat up!
Bats become super active hunters around June 21 because there are tons of insects flying around in the warm evening air.
